learning_outcomes.description: Analyse global challenges in agriculture, food, and rural development to identify their causes, interconnections, and implications.
Evaluate the role of your discipline in responding to complex global agricultural problems.
Communicate evidence-based insights clearly and persuasively to diverse audiences.
Justify ethical and socially responsible approaches to agricultural transformation.
Collaborate in interdisciplinary teams to explore sustainable agricultural responses.
Articulate your leadership potential to influence sustainable futures in agriculture and food systems.

assessments.task_description:
For this assessment, you will be working in groups of three or four. You will randomly be assigned to a group by the course coordinator in Week 1.

As a group, select one of a set of articles to be provided on Blackboard. These address an issue related to global food security, agroecosystem sustainability, and/or sustainable resource use. Prepare a critical analysis of the selected manuscript (3,500 words, excluding references) based on course lecture content and an independent review of relevant literature. In your critical analysis, you should:

Evaluate the article. Your assessment must be more than simply a summary. You should identify and explain the argument presented by the author(s).

Form your own objective opinion. Based on course lecture content and your own independent reading (review of relevant literature):

  • What assumptions underpin the authors' argument?
  • Which perspectives or stakeholders are represented or overlooked?
  • What evidence is convincing, uncertain, or contested?
  • How well do the proposed solutions address the complexity of the challenge?

What alternative argument would you put forth related to the subject of the selected article?

Support your opinion. Use references from relevant scientific literature to support your stated opinion.

Proposed structure:

  1. Introduction (200–300 words)
  2. Summary of the article's central argument (400–500 words)
  3. Critical evaluation of the argument (1,200 words)
  4. Integration with broader literature (900 words)
  5. Alternative perspective or recommendations (500–600 words)
  6. Conclusion (200 words)


All students in the group MUST submit a copy of the report - all group members must be clearly identified on the assessment coversheet.

How to write a critical analysis

Some information about how to write a critical analysis / critical review paper is available at:

  • http://depts.washington.edu/pswrite/Handouts/CriticalAnalysisPapers.pdf
  • https://student.unsw.edu.au/writing-critical-review


A review of relevant refereed literature

A review of relevant refereed literature is part of this assignment. Do not rely on web pages alone as this will limit the rigour of your review. References (in order of importance) might include:

  1. Review papers in peer reviewed journals – very useful; recent reviews will have numerous references you can follow up.
  2. Original articles in peer reviewed journals.
  3. Academic books – also will have references you can follow up.
  4. Popular science articles (e.g. Australian Cotton or Grain growers magazines; online science news The Conversation).
  5. Government agency and university websites. Government (.gov.au), CSIRO (csiro.au), University (.edu in the US or .edu.au) etc. are credible web resources.
  6. Popular websites. In general, avoid popular websites because they do not contain original information and what is presented has generally been re-interpreted. Some of the better sites tell you where the information comes from and you can use the popular site to find original source material.

Please use APA 7th referencing style https://guides.library.uq.edu.au/referencing/apa6

Any criterion not addressed in the assignment will earn 0% (0 marks). See the attached critical analysis assessment matrix for additional detail.

Acknowledging and/or referencing AI use 

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly acknowledge (by assessment coversheet) any use of AI or MT in each instance.  

A failure to acknowledge generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.  

The use of AI or MT must be acknowledged appropriately. Please see assessment guidelines in blackboard course site for specific instructions. Students may also refer to Library guides found under Additional assessment information > Acknowledging and referencing AI use.

assessments.task_description:
In addition to the group report, each student must submit an individual reflection (approximately 500 words).

The reflection provides an opportunity to critically consider your own learning throughout the process of completing the assessment. Rather than summarising the group report, you should reflect on how engaging with the selected article, the broader literature, and discussions within your group influenced your understanding of the global challenge.

Your reflection should address the following questions:

  • How did your understanding of the issue evolve during the assessment?
  • Which course concepts or theories most influenced your thinking?
  • Were there perspectives, assumptions or trade-offs that you had not previously considered?
  • How did discussions within your group challenge or reinforce your own views?
  • What insights from this assessment will influence how you approach complex global challenges in the future?

The reflection should demonstrate thoughtful engagement with your learning rather than simply describing the group's activities.

Acknowledging and/or referencing AI use 

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly acknowledge (by assessment coversheet) any use of AI or MT in each instance.  

A failure to acknowledge generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.  

The use of AI or MT must be acknowledged appropriately. Please see assessment guidelines in blackboard course site for specific instructions. Students may also refer to Library guides found under Additional assessment information > Acknowledging and referencing AI use.

assessments.task_description:
This is a sequential assessment item, undertaken as an online time-limited activity.

Students will need to attend the tutorial in person (attendance will be taken - please bring your student ID card) to complete the online tests, or a mark will not be awarded. Students are required to bring their own laptop/device to the lecture to be able to complete the assessment item. Please ensure your device is fully charged. Loan laptops from teaching staff are not available – should you require a loan laptop, please contact the Library.

Students will be required to download Safe Exam Browser (SEB), details will be discussed during the Week 1 lecture.

  • Practice online test (worth 0%) is to be completed in the week 5 tutorial session, to ensure all software is working correctly before Test 1 and Test 2. Students who have issues with SEB/Inspera during this trial test will need to contact ITS for assistance. The laptop/device used for the Practice test MUST be the same laptop/device that students intend to use for Tests 1 and 2.
  • Sequential Online Test 1 - This first online test will cover the fundamental content of the research module delivered from weeks 1 to 5. The online test will be held during the first hour of the Week 6 tutorial session (Monday 31 August 2026, 10:00–11:00 am), during scheduled class time, and will be invigilated. Students who arrive late to the lecture will not be given any additional time to complete Test 1.
  • Sequential Online Test 2 - This second online test will assess the discipline-focused research methodologies covered in weeks 6 to 10. This online test will be held during the first hour of the Week 11 tutorial session (Monday 12 October 2026, 10:00–11:00 am), during scheduled class time, and will be invigilated. Students who arrive late to the lecture will not be given any additional time to complete Test 2.


Each online test consists of multiple-choice questions and will contribute 20% to your final mark. You will have 45 minutes to complete each test.

Additional details will be provided on the course Blackboard site.

This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempt to use AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.task_description:
Select one of the government policy documents listed on Blackboard that addresses a major global challenge related to agriculture, food systems, water, climate change, biodiversity, sustainability, or the food–water–energy nexus. You will prepare a written policy critique that critically evaluates the selected policy using concepts, theories, and evidence introduced throughout the course.

Policies are statements of principles or courses of action adopted (or proposed) by governments or organisations. They guide decision-making and shape responses to complex societal issues. In this course, you will focus on public policy related to global agricultural and environmental challenges. Policies may also be referred to as strategies, plans, frameworks, or schemes.

A defining characteristic of policy is that it “provides the rules of engagement as well as steering a course for future generations” (Haigh 2012). Public policies are intended to improve societal outcomes and are often shaped by scientific evidence, political priorities, stakeholder interests, ethical considerations, and institutional constraints.

You will prepare a policy critique of approximately 3,000 words (excluding references). Your critique should draw on:

  • course lecture material,
  • independent academic literature,
  • systems thinking,
  • and critical analysis.


Your critique should address the following components:

  1. Policy Context and Purpose: Outline the policy issue and the intended outcomes of the selected policy. Provide relevant background and context, which may include:
  • historical development,
  • political or institutional context,
  • key stakeholders,
  • implementation challenges,
  • and any significant revisions or controversies.

What does the responsible government body hope to achieve through this policy?

2. Scientific and Evidence Base: Critically assess the scientific basis of the selected policy using peer-reviewed literature and other credible evidence sources.

Consider:

  • Does the policy demonstrate strong links between science and decision-making?
  • Is the policy aligned with the best available scientific knowledge?
  • Where is the policy scientifically defensible?
  • Where could it be strengthened through greater integration of scientific evidence?


3. Alignment with Sustainability and the SDGs: Evaluate how well the selected policy aligns with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

You may consider:

  • synergies between goals,
  • trade-offs,
  • unintended consequences,
  • and tensions between environmental, social, and economic priorities.


4. Long-Term Sustainability Implications: Evaluate the potential long-term sustainability implications of implementing the selected policy.

Your discussion may include:

  • implications for food security,
  • impacts on energy use or water allocation,
  • environmental sustainability,
  • equity and social justice considerations,
  • resilience,
  • and the influence of globalisation or international markets.

You should critically examine how the policy may affect interconnected agricultural, environmental, and social systems over time.

5. Policy recommendations and pathways to change

Based on your analysis, critically evaluate how the selected policy could be strengthened.

Your discussion should consider:

  • proposed policy improvements;
  • opportunities to strengthen sustainability outcomes;
  • governance and institutional arrangements;
  • leadership and collaboration required for implementation;
  • barriers, trade-offs and unintended consequences;
  • the role of evidence, communication and interdisciplinary practice in supporting policy change.

Drawing on course concepts, justify your recommendations and explain how they could contribute to more sustainable agricultural and food systems.

Referencing

Please use APA 7th referencing style (see UQ Library guidelines).

References

DAFF 2013, National Food Plan: Our Food Future, Department of Agriculture, Fisheries and Forestry, Canberra.

Haigh, Y. 2012, Public Policy in Australia: Theory and Practice, Oxford University Press, Melbourne.

Any criterion not addressed in the assignment will earn 0% (0 marks).

Acknowledging and/or referencing AI use 

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly acknowledge (by assessment coversheet) any use of AI or MT in each instance.  

A failure to acknowledge generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.  

The use of AI or MT must be acknowledged appropriately. Please see assessment guidelines in blackboard course site for specific instructions. Students may also refer to Library guides found under Additional assessment information > Acknowledging and referencing AI use.
